_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Functions
summary: Name a reusable task, give it inputs, and return a result.
refresher: A C++ function writes its return type before its name and gives every parameter a type. For example, `int square(int n)` accepts an integer and returns an integer. Use `void` when no value is returned.

blocks.body: A function gives a name to a reusable task. Its parameters receive input values, and `return` sends a result back to the caller. In C++, the function also states the type of each input and the type of its result.

Read `int larger(int a, int b)` from left to right: the function returns an `int`, its name is `larger`, and it receives two integer parameters. A call such as `larger(7, 4)` fills those parameters and waits for the returned answer.

blocks.title: C++ types are the main difference from Python here
blocks.body: Python writes `def square(n):`. C++ writes `int square(int n)`. Both name the function and its input, but C++ also states that the input and result are integers.

blocks.caption: The arguments 7 and 4 fill a and b. The function returns 7, and main prints it.
A non-void function must use return to send its answer back.

blocks.body: The first version calculates a value but throws it away. The corrected version returns the result to the line that called the function. Make sure every possible path in a non-void function returns the promised type.

blocks.title: Return the promised result
blocks.body: If a function begins with a type such as `int` or `double`, the caller expects a result of that type. Use `return` on every possible path. A `void` function performs work without returning a value.

blocks.prompt: What is the return type here?
blocks.why: The type written before the function name declares the returned value type.
The argument initializes n, and the returned multiplication result is six.

blocks.title: Functions checklist
items: Match each parameter type to its input.
Return the promised type on every path.
Give the function one clear purpose.
Use void only when no result is needed.
